news_summary: Today's space news highlights breakthroughs in understanding cosmic signals, advancements in sustainable lunar habitat technology, insights into the universe through electromagnetic spectrum research, and opportunities for public participation in studying star formation.
news_summary_fr: L'actualité spatiale d'aujourd'hui met en avant des avancées majeures dans la compréhension des signaux cosmiques, des progrès dans le domaine des technologies d'habitats lunaires durables, de nouvelles perspectives sur l'univers grâce à la recherche sur le spectre électromagnétique, ainsi que des possibilités pour le grand public de participer à l'étude de la formation des étoiles.
news_summary_es: Las noticias espaciales de hoy destacan avances en la comprensión de las señales cósmicas, progresos en la tecnología de hábitats lunares sostenibles, nuevos conocimientos sobre el universo gracias a la investigación del espectro electromagnético y oportunidades para la participación del público en el estudio de la formación estelar.

articles.title: A stellar “Rosetta stone” reveals the source of mysterious cosmic signals
articles.summary: Astronomers have finally cracked the mystery behind a strange class of repeating cosmic signals that has baffled scientists for years. Using Australia’s ASKAP radio telescope, researchers traced the bursts to a rare stellar duo in which a dense white dwarf is relentlessly siphoning material from a nearby red dwarf companion. As the stolen matter spirals inward, the system unleashes powerful radio waves and X-rays every 1.4 hours.
articles.content:
Astronomers have traced a mysterious type of repeating cosmic signal to an unusual pair of stars, providing the strongest evidence yet for the source of one of astronomy's most puzzling phenomena.

The discovery was made by an international research team led by scientists at the University of Sydney using CSIRO's ASKAP radio telescope. Their findings identify the origin of a rare class of objects known as long-period radio transients, mysterious bursts of radio waves that have puzzled astronomers since they were first detected in only a handful of locations across the Milky Way.

The results were published in Nature Astronomy.

Lead author Kovi Rose, a PhD student in the University of Sydney's School of Physics and CSIRO, said the team was finally able to connect one of these enigmatic signals to a specific type of stellar system.

"For the first time we have pinpointed the origin of these signals, confirming the source to be a 'cataclysmic variable', or an accreting white dwarf star," said Mr. Rose.

"Long-period radio transients have puzzled astronomers for years," Mr. Rose said. "We've only found about a dozen, and their origins have been unclear. Now, we've been able to show that the source for one of these transients comes from a white dwarf actively pulling material from a companion star."

Rare White Dwarf System Revealed

The newly identified system, known as ASKAP J1745−5051, consists of a white dwarf and a red dwarf locked in an extremely close orbit. A white dwarf is the dense remnant of a dead star, roughly the size of Earth but with a mass comparable to that of the Sun. Its companion is a much larger but less dense red dwarf star containing about one-tenth the Sun's mass.

The two stars circle one another in just over an hour.

As the white dwarf pulls gas from its companion, the material heats up and emits X-rays. At the same time, interactions between the stars' magnetic fields generate powerful radio bursts. Together, these emissions repeat on a regular cycle every 1.4 hours.

"These emissions are all tied to the orbital motion of the system," Mr. Rose said. "But interestingly, the radio and X-ray signals don't peak at the same time, which tells us they're being produced in different regions of the system."

The researchers found that the radio waves are likely produced where the stars' magnetic fields collide and interact with the stream of charged material flowing toward the white dwarf. This process creates tightly focused bursts of radiation that sweep through space.

Solving the Long-Period Radio Transient Mystery

When long-period radio transients were first discovered, many astronomers suspected they might be unusually slow-spinning neutron stars known as pulsars. However, existing models suggest neutron stars rotating that slowly should not be capable of producing these signals.

The new findings support a different explanation. At least some long-period radio transients appear to originate in binary star systems involving white dwarfs.

"Some similar objects had been linked to binary systems before, but this is the first one where we can clearly see both stars and the accretion process in action," said Professor Murphy, Head of School at the University of Sydney School of Physics and Chief Investigator at the ARC Centre of Excellence for Gravitational Wave Discovery (OzGrav).

The system is also only the second known long-period radio transient found to produce regular X-rays. It is the first example in which scientists have confirmed exactly what is causing the periodic behavior.

A Cosmic Rosetta Stone

Researchers believe ASKAP J1745−5051 could become an important reference object for understanding other mysterious radio transients.

The system was discovered using ASKAP, a radio telescope owned and operated by CSIRO, Australia's national science agency. ASKAP combines a wide field of view, high resolution, and exceptional sensitivity, making it particularly effective at detecting unusual signals that might otherwise go unnoticed.

"This system gives us a way to decode these signals. It could help us determine whether other long-period transients are more like pulsars or like white dwarf systems, acting like a stellar Rosetta stone," said Mr. Rose, referring to the archaeological object discovered in Egypt that helped translate ancient hieroglyphics.

Beyond helping explain mysterious radio signals, the system also offers scientists a rare opportunity to study extreme physical conditions that cannot be recreated in laboratories on Earth.

"These systems are natural laboratories," Mr. Rose said. "They allow us to test our understanding of how matter behaves in strong magnetic fields and under intense gravitational forces."

Future Observations Planned

The team plans to continue studying the system using radio, optical, and X-ray telescopes. By combining observations across different wavelengths, they hope to better understand how these signals are produced and whether similar mechanisms can explain the broader population of long-period radio transients.

"Each new discovery is helping us piece together the bigger picture," Mr. Rose said. "We're only just beginning to understand this new class of cosmic events."

The international collaboration included researchers from Australia, the United States, China, Canada, Spain, and Israel. Observations were carried out using CSIRO's ASKAP and Australia Telescope Compact Array in Australia, the MeerKAT radio telescope in South Africa, the SOAR and Magellan optical telescopes in Chile, and the space-based Swift (UV/X-ray) and Einstein Probe (X-ray) observatories.

The authors reported no competing interests. Funding for the research was provided by the Australian Research Council Centre of Excellence for Gravitational Wave Discovery (OzGrav), NASA, the Alfred P. Sloan Foundation, the Professor Harry Messel Research Fellowship in Physics Endowment, the European Research Council, and the China Scholarship Council.
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant scientific breakthrough in astronomy where researchers identified the source of mysterious long-period radio transients as a rare binary star system involving a white dwarf and a red dwarf. This discovery solves a long-standing cosmic mystery, has broad implications for understanding stellar phenomena, and provides a natural laboratory for studying extreme physical conditions, benefiting the scientific community and humanity's knowledge of the universe.
articles.personality_title: Astronomers identify rare star duo as source of mysterious cosmic signals
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – For many years, astronomers have detected strange bursts of radio waves from space called long-period radio transients. These signals repeated regularly but their exact source was unknown, puzzling scientists.

**What happened** – A team led by the University of Sydney used Australia’s ASKAP radio telescope to trace one of these signals to a rare pair of stars. The system, named ASKAP J1745−5051, includes a white dwarf star pulling material from a nearby red dwarf star. This process causes the system to emit powerful bursts of radio waves and X-rays every 1.4 hours.

**Impact** – This discovery is the first time scientists have clearly identified the source of these mysterious signals. It shows that some long-period radio transients come from white dwarfs stealing gas from companion stars, rather than from neutron stars as previously thought. The system acts like a “Rosetta stone,” helping scientists understand other similar signals and study extreme space conditions.

**What’s next step** – Researchers will continue observing this star pair with different telescopes to learn more about how these signals form. Their work could reveal if other mysterious cosmic signals come from similar star systems and improve our knowledge of stellar physics.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Scientists have solved a cosmic mystery by linking unusual repeating radio signals to a rare pair of stars, opening new paths to study the universe.
articles.personality_title_fr: Des astronomes identifient un duo d’étoiles rare comme source de signaux cosmiques mystérieux
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Depuis plusieurs années, des astronomes détectent des signaux étranges sous forme d’éclats d’ondes radio qui se répètent, appelés transitoires radio à longue période. Leur origine exacte restait inconnue, ce qui intriguait les scientifiques.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Une équipe dirigée par l’Université de Sydney a utilisé le radiotélescope ASKAP en Australie pour localiser un de ces signaux. Ils ont découvert qu’il provenait d’un système rare composé d’une naine blanche qui vole du gaz à une étoile naine rouge proche. Ce vol de matière crée des émissions puissantes d’ondes radio et de rayons X toutes les 1,4 heures.

**Impact** – C’est la première fois que les scientifiques identifient clairement la source de ces signaux mystérieux. Cela montre que certains transitoires radio à longue période viennent de naines blanches et non d’étoiles à neutrons, comme on le pensait auparavant. Ce système agit comme une « pierre de Rosette », aidant à comprendre d’autres signaux similaires et à étudier des conditions extrêmes dans l’espace.

**Prochaine étape** – Les chercheurs vont continuer à observer ce duo d’étoiles avec différents télescopes pour mieux comprendre la formation de ces signaux. Leur travail pourrait révéler si d’autres signaux cosmiques mystérieux proviennent de systèmes similaires et améliorer nos connaissances sur la physique stellaire.

**Conclusion en une phrase** – Les scientifiques ont résolu un mystère cosmique en reliant des signaux radio inhabituels à un duo d’étoiles rare, ouvrant de nouvelles voies pour étudier l’univers.
articles.personality_title_es: Astrónomos identifican un raro dúo estelar como fuente de señales cósmicas misteriosas
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Durante años, los astrónomos han detectado extrañas ráfagas de ondas de radio que se repiten llamadas transitorios de radio de largo período. El origen exacto de estas señales era desconocido y confundía a los científicos.

**Qué pasó** – Un equipo liderado por la Universidad de Sídney usó el radiotelescopio ASKAP de Australia para localizar una de estas señales. Descubrieron que proviene de un sistema raro formado por una enana blanca que extrae material de una estrella enana roja cercana. Este proceso genera potentes emisiones de ondas de radio y rayos X cada 1.4 horas.

**Impacto** – Esta es la primera vez que los científicos identifican claramente la fuente de estas señales misteriosas. Demuestra que algunos transitorios de radio de largo período provienen de enanas blancas y no de estrellas de neutrones, como se pensaba antes. Este sistema funciona como una “piedra de Rosetta”, ayudando a entender otras señales similares y a estudiar condiciones extremas en el espacio.

**Próximo paso** – Los investigadores continuarán observando este par de estrellas con diferentes telescopios para aprender más sobre cómo se forman estas señales. Su trabajo podría revelar si otras señales cósmicas misteriosas vienen de sistemas similares y mejorar nuestro conocimiento de la física estelar.

**Conclusión en una frase** – Los científicos resolvieron un misterio cósmico al relacionar señales de radio inusuales con un raro dúo estelar, abriendo nuevas formas de estudiar el universo.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm-toned painting of two intertwined stars—a small, dense white dwarf glowing softly with a cool, pale light, and a larger, warm red dwarf emitting a gentle reddish hue—connected by a flowing, luminous stream of glowing material spiraling from the red dwarf to the white dwarf, set against a calm cosmic backdrop with subtle waves of radio and X-ray energy radiating in distinct, overlapping patterns around them.

articles.title: NASA Testing Wastewater Treatment Facility for Future Moon Base
articles.summary: A mobile wastewater treatment system built at NASA’s Kennedy Space Center in Florida that can help prepare for long-duration missions on the Moon and Mars departed the spaceport and arrived at the University of North Dakota in Grand Forks. Graduate students at the university will test the technology under conditions designed to closely mimic the […]
articles.content:
Technicians prepare the Divergent Deployable Wastewater Treatment Facility, designed to turn crew wastewater into useful resources, for transport at NASA’s Kennedy Space Center in Florida on Tuesday, April 21, 2026. NASA/Kim Shiflett

A mobile wastewater treatment system built at NASA’s Kennedy Space Center in Florida that can help prepare for long-duration missions on the Moon and Mars departed the spaceport and arrived at the University of North Dakota in Grand Forks. Graduate students at the university will test the technology under conditions designed to closely mimic the challenges of operating on another planetary surface.

The Divergent Deployable Wastewater Treatment Facility is designed to turn crew wastewater into useful resources, which future explorers will need every day. At the University of North Dakota, teams will integrate this new wastewater system with the university’s Integrated Lunar/Martian Analog Habitat. Student operators and NASA researchers will study how the facility performs when connected to a habitat-like environment and exposed to the kinds of operational limits crews could face on another planet.

“NASA’s Artemis program is laying the groundwork for a sustained human presence on the Moon, where habitats will need to operate far from the steady resupply chain that supports astronauts in partial gravity,” said Luke Roberson, surface water systems lead within the Mars Campaign Office at NASA Kennedy. “To solve that challenge, we are developing the future of sustainable lunar surface systems to process wastewater into nutrient feedstocks for plants and biomanufacturing.”

How Treatment System Works

Housed inside an 8.5-by-24-foot trailer, the facility brings together three biological reactor systems, a vertical garden, water-polishing hardware, environmental monitoring, autonomous control software, and safety systems. The trailer was outfitted at NASA Kennedy to function as a deployable laboratory and to travel between at least two simulation test sites as the technology matures.

Unlike wastewater systems on Earth, this facility keeps waste streams separate. That divergent approach is important for small crews, because wastewater from four to eight people can be highly concentrated. Urine, hygiene water, laundry water, fecal waste, and food waste each contain different levels of salts, solids, carbon, nitrogen, phosphorus, and other compounds. Treating them separately allows each stream to be processed by the reactor best suited for the job.

To do that, the system uses three different bioreactors to treat waste streams. The Anaerobic Phototrophic Membrane Bioreactor processes fecal and food waste and converts it into a nutrient-rich wastewater that can support plant growth. The Suspended Aerobic Membrane Bioreactor processes urine and flush water. The Membrane Aerated Biological Reactor treats graywater from hygiene and laundry activities. Collectively, the bioreactors process nutrients to feed the facility’s vertical garden and prepare the water for reuse. Inside that garden, crops will grow hydroponically, or without using soil, by using nutrient solutions derived from the bioreactors. Researchers will compare crop performance with plants grown using standard hydroponic nutrients.

NASA’s Dr. Roberson demonstrating the Divergent Wastewater Treatment Facility to UND Chair Dr. De Leon and Dr. Robert Kraus, Dean of UND’s School of Aerospace Sciences. University of North Dakota

At North Dakota, under a NASA EPSCoR (Established Program to Stimulate Competitive Research) grant, the facility was connected to the Integrated Lunar/Martian Analog Habitat through a bathroom interface that includes a urine-diverting toilet. The setup will allow different waste streams to be separated at the source and sent to the correct treatment systems. In parallel, Ali Alshami’s team is developing novel membrane-based separation technologies intended for future integration into the divergent wastewater facility to improve water recovery efficiency, contaminant rejection, and overall system resilience for long-duration habitation missions.

“The tests will help NASA evaluate real-world operation, crew training needs, system reliability, and how wastewater simulants compare with actual human metabolic waste in an analog mission environment,” said Alshami.

These efforts are focused on advancing compact, energy-efficient treatment approaches capable of handling complex wastewater streams generated in closed-loop extraterrestrial environments.

“The testing campaign at the University of North Dakota supports the facility’s technology maturation from laboratory-scale validation toward demonstration in a relevant Inflatable Lunar/Martian Analog Habitat environment,” said Pablo De Leon, professor and department chair of Space Studies at the University of North Dakota.

Lessons learned could inform future higher-fidelity tests, including potential integration with NASA’s next generation of yearlong simulated Mars missions via isolation analogs at the agency’s Johnson Space Center in Houston.

Technology for Making Moon Base Sustainable

The work is part of NASA’s broader Bioregenerative Life Support Systems effort, which is developing biological approaches to reduce dependence on Earth-supplied consumables. In future lunar or Martian habitats, systems like the wastewater treatment facility could help close life support loops by recovering water, recycling nutrients, supporting crop production, and reducing the amount of waste that must be stored or discarded. Further NASA research completed trade studies demonstrating how bioregenerative life support becomes more effective for space travel over current life support technologies.

NASA researchers also are exploring how wastewater-recovered resources could support in-space manufacturing. One effort is studying how nutrient-rich water from bioregenerative wastewater systems could feed microbes that produce lactic acid, which can be turned into polylactic acid. The material could one day serve as a binder for 3D printing with lunar or Martian regolith, the loose, fragmental surface material, or could be used for replacement parts, extending the value of recovered waste beyond water and food systems.

“By sending the facility from NASA Kennedy to North Dakota, the agency is moving a key part of that circular economy out of the lab and into a real-world test,” said J.J. Edelmann, surface systems domain lead for the Mars Campaign Office at NASA Headquarters in Washington. “The work may begin with wastewater, but its goal is much larger. We want to help future crews live sustainably on the Moon, learn how to operate farther from Earth, and carry those lessons forward to Mars.”

To learn more about the agency’s lunar and Mars exploration, visit:

https://nasa.gov/esdmd
articles.reasoning: The article describes a significant technological advancement with real-world impact for sustainable long-duration human space exploration on the Moon and Mars. It details the development and testing of a mobile wastewater treatment system that recycles crew waste into useful resources, supporting closed-loop life support systems. The story has broad significance for future space missions and includes substantial technical and contextual information.
articles.personality_title: NASA tests new wastewater system to support future Moon and Mars missions
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – NASA is preparing for long missions on the Moon and Mars, where astronauts will need to live and work far from Earth. One big challenge is managing wastewater, which includes urine, hygiene water, and food waste. Recycling this waste into useful resources will help crews survive longer without needing constant supplies from Earth.

**What happened** – NASA built a mobile wastewater treatment system called the Divergent Deployable Wastewater Treatment Facility. It was developed at Kennedy Space Center in Florida and recently moved to the University of North Dakota. There, graduate students are testing it in a special habitat that mimics living on the Moon or Mars. The system separates different waste types and uses three bioreactors to clean and recycle the water and nutrients, which then help grow plants in a vertical garden.

**Impact** – This system is unique because it treats each type of wastewater separately, making it more efficient for small crews. It turns waste into clean water and plant nutrients, supporting a closed-loop life support system. This means astronauts could recycle their waste to grow food and reuse water, reducing the need to bring supplies from Earth. Testing it in a real-like habitat helps NASA learn how the system works in space-like conditions and how crews might operate it.

**What's next step** – The University of North Dakota team will continue testing the system with real habitat conditions and improve it with new technologies for better water recovery. NASA plans to use lessons from these tests to prepare for longer missions, including yearlong Mars simulations at Johnson Space Center. This work will help make future Moon and Mars bases more sustainable.

**One-sentence takeaway** – NASA’s new wastewater treatment system recycles crew waste into water and nutrients, supporting sustainable life support for future Moon and Mars missions.
articles.personality_title_fr: La NASA teste un nouveau système de traitement des eaux usées pour les missions lunaires et martiennes
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – La NASA se prépare à des missions longues sur la Lune et Mars, où les astronautes devront vivre loin de la Terre. Un grand défi est de gérer les eaux usées, comme l’urine, l’eau de toilette et les déchets alimentaires. Recycler ces déchets en ressources utiles aidera les équipages à survivre plus longtemps sans avoir besoin d’approvisionnements constants.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – La NASA a construit un système mobile de traitement des eaux usées appelé l’installation Divergent Deployable Wastewater Treatment Facility. Développé au Kennedy Space Center en Floride, il a récemment été transféré à l’Université du Dakota du Nord. Là-bas, des étudiants de troisième cycle le testent dans un habitat qui imite la vie sur la Lune ou Mars. Le système sépare les différents types de déchets et utilise trois bioréacteurs pour purifier et recycler l’eau et les nutriments, qui servent ensuite à faire pousser des plantes dans un jardin vertical.

**Impact** – Ce système est unique car il traite chaque type d’eau usée séparément, ce qui le rend plus efficace pour de petits équipages. Il transforme les déchets en eau propre et en nutriments pour les plantes, soutenant un système de vie en circuit fermé. Cela signifie que les astronautes pourraient recycler leurs déchets pour cultiver leur nourriture et réutiliser l’eau, réduisant ainsi les besoins d’apport depuis la Terre. Les tests dans un habitat réaliste aident la NASA à comprendre comment le système fonctionne dans des conditions proches de l’espace et comment les équipages pourraient l’utiliser.

**Prochaine étape** – L’équipe de l’Université du Dakota du Nord continuera à tester le système dans des conditions d’habitat réelles et à l’améliorer avec de nouvelles technologies pour une meilleure récupération de l’eau. La NASA prévoit d’utiliser les leçons de ces tests pour préparer des missions plus longues, y compris des simulations d’un an sur Mars au Johnson Space Center. Ce travail aidera à rendre les futures bases lunaires et martiennes plus durables.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Le nouveau système de traitement des eaux usées de la NASA recycle les déchets des équipages en eau et nutriments, soutenant un système de vie durable pour les futures missions lunaires et martiennes.
articles.personality_title_es: La NASA prueba un nuevo sistema de tratamiento de aguas residuales para misiones en la Luna y Marte
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – La NASA se está preparando para misiones largas en la Luna y Marte, donde los astronautas deberán vivir lejos de la Tierra. Un gran reto es manejar las aguas residuales, que incluyen orina, agua de higiene y desechos de comida. Reciclar estos residuos en recursos útiles ayudará a los equipos a sobrevivir más tiempo sin depender de suministros constantes.

**Qué pasó** – La NASA construyó un sistema móvil de tratamiento de aguas residuales llamado Divergent Deployable Wastewater Treatment Facility. Fue desarrollado en el Kennedy Space Center en Florida y recientemente se trasladó a la Universidad de Dakota del Norte. Allí, estudiantes de posgrado lo están probando en un hábitat que simula las condiciones de vivir en la Luna o Marte. El sistema separa los diferentes tipos de residuos y usa tres biorreactores para limpiar y reciclar el agua y nutrientes, que luego ayudan a cultivar plantas en un jardín vertical.

**Impacto** – Este sistema es único porque trata cada tipo de agua residual por separado, lo que lo hace más eficiente para tripulaciones pequeñas. Convierte los residuos en agua limpia y nutrientes para plantas, apoyando un sistema de soporte vital cerrado. Esto significa que los astronautas podrían reciclar sus residuos para cultivar alimentos y reutilizar el agua, reduciendo la necesidad de traer suministros desde la Tierra. Probarlo en un hábitat realista ayuda a la NASA a entender cómo funcionaría en condiciones espaciales y cómo los equipos podrían operarlo.

**Próximo paso** – El equipo de la Universidad de Dakota del Norte seguirá probando el sistema en condiciones reales de hábitat y mejorándolo con nuevas tecnologías para aumentar la recuperación de agua. La NASA planea usar las lecciones de estas pruebas para preparar misiones más largas, incluyendo simulaciones de un año en Marte en el Johnson Space Center. Este trabajo ayudará a que las futuras bases en la Luna y Marte sean más sostenibles.

**Conclusión en una frase** – El nuevo sistema de tratamiento de aguas residuales de la NASA recicla los residuos de la tripulación en agua y nutrientes, apoyando un soporte vital sostenible para futuras misiones en la Luna y Marte.
articles.image_prompt: A warm, detailed painting of a compact, futuristic mobile laboratory trailer with softly glowing bioreactor chambers visible through semi-transparent walls, connected by tubes to a lush vertical hydroponic garden thriving inside a transparent dome, set against a gentle landscape blending a lunar surface with hints of a university campus in the background, all rendered in natural, earthy tones and soft light.

articles.title: How the electromagnetic spectrum opened our eyes to the universe
articles.summary: Our understanding of the electromagnetic spectrum goes back to Isaac Newton, but astronomers are still finding new ways to employ it. Astrophysicist Emma Chapman explores how much these invisible waves can reveal to us about the cosmos – and whether they might show us that we’re not alone
articles.content:
Our understanding of the electromagnetic spectrum goes back to Isaac Newton, but astronomers are still finding new ways to employ it. Astrophysicist Emma Chapman explores how much these invisible waves can reveal to us about the cosmos – and whether they might show us that we’re not alone

Telescopes at the Square Kilometre Array SKAO

The following is an extract from our Lost in Space-Time newsletter. Each month, we dive into fascinating ideas from around the universe. You can sign up for Lost in Space-Time here.

My first encounter with invisible light came in my early years, and I thought it was magic. Radios filled every room of my childhood home: the kitchen, bedrooms, even the hallway. I would slowly turn the dial on older sets, listening as music and voices emerged from the static before fading away again as I surfed the radio waves. Long before I understood I was tuning into part of the electromagnetic spectrum, I felt the wonder of sensing something my eyes couldn’t see.

Human eyes evolved to detect only a narrow band of light – enough to navigate landscapes and recognise danger – but the universe shines across a vast spectrum stretching from gamma rays to radio waves. Different wavelengths of light interact with matter in different ways, meaning each reveals a different side of the world, and universe, around us. We encounter these properties constantly in everyday life. Microwaves, for example, are just the right energy to excite water molecules, perfect for the noble application of reheating last night’s leftovers. X-rays, meanwhile, have just enough energy to pass through soft tissue, but are absorbed by bone, allowing doctors to image our skeletons.

Radio light is the longest wavelength and lowest energy light in the electromagnetic spectrum, able to travel enormous distances largely unimpeded, and pass relatively easily through Earth’s atmosphere. This makes radio waves a powerful medium for communication on Earth, as I experienced as a child, but they are also an ideal messenger from the distant reaches of space and time. Years later, as my interests turned towards cosmology, it felt fitting that I would end up using radio telescopes to study the universe’s first stars and galaxies.

The electromagnetic spectrum as we know it today follows centuries of scientific discovery, as researchers gradually discovered that the universe extended far beyond the limits of human vision. It started with a rainbow in 1665, when Isaac Newton used glass prisms to show that white light could be split into a spectrum of colours, from red to violet. By 1800, astronomer William Herschel had discovered infrared light, again with a prism, by measuring the temperature of different colours of light and noticing that his thermometer ticked higher just beyond the red end of the spectrum. By the end of the 19th century, advances in electromagnetism and laboratory technology had revealed radio waves, microwaves, X-rays, and gamma rays, completing our modern view of the spectrum.

Subscriber-only newsletter Sign up to Lost in Space-Time Untangle mind-bending physics, maths and the weirdness of reality with our monthly, special-guest-written newsletter. Sign up to newsletter

Invisible made visible

Optical astronomy is as ancient as civilisation itself, born from the simple fact that we arrive into this world already equipped to see sunlight or starlight. Other regions of the spectrum require additional tools: antennas and dishes for radio waves and microwaves, and specialised detectors for X-rays and infrared light. We can think of each of these subcategories as languages where, to understand the universe, we need the ability to translate to the optical light our eyes more naturally comprehend or, in the case of household radios, sounds our ears can appreciate. Only then are we rewarded with a complete cosmos of unseen messages and hidden histories.

We need the entire spectrum to fully illuminate the cosmos. UV light, for example, traces water plumes erupting from the surface of the smallest of Jupiter’s Galilean moons, Europa. The strong magnetic fields enveloping the giant planet interact with the atmosphere of the orbiting moon, generating aurora that shine brightly in the ultraviolet wavelengths. As water vapour from the plumes rises into the atmosphere, it temporarily alters the brightness of the aurora. Observing this allows astronomers to infer the presence and composition of the material erupting from the potentially habitable ocean below Europa’s icy surface.

Composite images show a suspected plume of material erupting two years apart from the same location on Jupiter’s icy moon Europa NASA, ESA, W. Sparks (STScI), and the USGS Astrogeology Science Center

And for the infrared, we have the James Webb Space Telescope (JWST), which sits 1.5 million kilometres from Earth, shielded from the sun by a sunshade the size of a tennis court. With the clearest and coldest view of the universe ever achieved, JWST has been rewriting what we thought we knew about the how the first stars and galaxies formed.

As the universe expands, light from the early galaxies is shifted into longer, infrared wavelengths – it moves towards the red end of the spectrum, so we say it is redshifted – which goes on to be adeptly captured by JWST. With a simple translation, labelling infrared wavelengths with optical colours as if completing a paint-by-numbers, we see galaxies just as they were just a few hundred million years after the big bang. Fascinating for sure, but there is a problem. A lot of these galaxies look more middle-aged then youthful – they are simply too big to be explained by star formation and galaxy evolution as we thought we understood it. How did they grow so fast?

To answer that question, astronomers are gathering older light that has been shifted into even longer wavelengths: radio waves that have travelled further, for even longer. Headquartered at Jodrell Bank Observatory in the UK, the Square Kilometre Array (SKA) will consist in part of over 100,000 antennas spread across the Western Australian outback into one enormous radio observatory, able to hear the faintest whispers from only a few tens of millions of years after the big bang. By detecting faint signals from the hydrogen gas that swirled around the primordial universe, the SKA aims to translate messages from the very first civilisation of stars and baby black holes. This is only one scientific application for the SKA, however. It will observe a multitude of celestial phenomena, mapping the furthest arms of the Milky Way, for example, and listening for signs of extraterrestrial intelligence.

The search for extraterrestrial life (SETI) is a research area which particularly fascinates me, because it beautifully demonstrates the complementary nature of observations at different wavelengths. With optical telescopes such as the Transiting Exoplanet Survey Satellite (TESS), we are cataloguing thousands of planets outside our solar system, by measuring the infinitesimal drop in brightness we observe when a planet passes in front of the star it orbits. Then, with infrared telescopes such as JWST, we can measure the composition of the exoplanetary atmosphere, and flag it as potentially habitable. Finally, with radio telescopes, we can target the shortlist of planets promising for hosting life and listen for extraterrestrial messages, whether an intentional greeting, or the unintentional leakage of radio communications like television broadcasts. After all, the laws of physics apply as much on exoplanets as they do on Earth, making radio the most obvious medium for communication. Perhaps one day, as we surf the radio waves from other star systems, an altogether alien voice will emerge from the static.

We are born fluent in only a single language of light, yet the universe is profoundly multilingual. The electromagnetic spectrum is a Rosetta Stone, allowing our telescopes to translate unseen stories written in invisible scripts. When read together, these stories allow us to tune into a universe far richer than the one our eyes alone can see.

Emma Chapman is an astrophysicist at the University of Nottingham, UK, and author of Radio Universe: How to Explore Space Without Leaving Earth (John Murray, 2026).
articles.reasoning: The article highlights significant scientific progress in astronomy through the use of the electromagnetic spectrum, enabling deeper understanding of the universe, early galaxies, and the search for extraterrestrial life. This has broad scientific and societal impact by expanding human knowledge and inspiring curiosity about our place in the cosmos.
articles.personality_title: How invisible light helps us explore the universe
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – For centuries, scientists have learned that light comes in many forms beyond what our eyes can see. This range of light, called the electromagnetic spectrum, includes radio waves, X-rays, and infrared light. Each type of light reveals different parts of the universe.

**What happened** – Astrophysicist Emma Chapman explains how modern telescopes use these invisible waves to study space. For example, the James Webb Space Telescope looks at infrared light to see distant galaxies, while the Square Kilometre Array, a huge radio telescope in Australia, will listen for signals from the universe’s first stars and even possible signs of alien life.

**Impact** – Using the full spectrum of light lets scientists discover things invisible to our eyes, like water plumes on Jupiter’s moon Europa or galaxies that formed much faster than expected. This approach helps us understand how the universe began and where life might exist beyond Earth.

**What’s next step** – The Square Kilometre Array will soon start gathering more detailed radio signals from space, allowing researchers to study the early universe and search for messages from other civilizations. Meanwhile, telescopes like James Webb will continue to reveal new details about distant stars and planets.

**One-sentence takeaway** – By using invisible light beyond what we can see, scientists are uncovering new stories about the universe’s past and the possibility of life beyond Earth.
articles.personality_title_fr: Comment la lumière invisible nous aide à explorer l’univers
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Depuis des siècles, les scientifiques savent que la lumière existe sous plusieurs formes invisibles à nos yeux. Ce large éventail, appelé spectre électromagnétique, comprend les ondes radio, les rayons X et la lumière infrarouge. Chaque type de lumière révèle une part différente de l’univers.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – L’astrophysicienne Emma Chapman explique comment les télescopes modernes utilisent ces ondes invisibles pour étudier l’espace. Par exemple, le télescope spatial James Webb observe la lumière infrarouge pour voir des galaxies lointaines, tandis que le Square Kilometre Array, un immense radiotélescope en Australie, écoutera les signaux des premières étoiles et même des indices de vie extraterrestre.

**Impact** – Utiliser tout le spectre lumineux permet aux scientifiques de découvrir des choses invisibles à nos yeux, comme des panaches d’eau sur Europe, une lune de Jupiter, ou des galaxies formées plus vite que prévu. Cette méthode aide à mieux comprendre le début de l’univers et où la vie pourrait exister ailleurs.

**Prochaine étape** – Le Square Kilometre Array commencera bientôt à capter des signaux radio plus précis de l’espace, permettant d’étudier l’univers primitif et de chercher des messages venus d’autres civilisations. Pendant ce temps, des télescopes comme James Webb continueront de révéler de nouveaux détails sur des étoiles et planètes lointaines.

**En une phrase** – En utilisant la lumière invisible au-delà de notre vue, les scientifiques découvrent de nouvelles histoires sur le passé de l’univers et la possibilité de vie au-delà de la Terre.
articles.personality_title_es: Cómo la luz invisible nos ayuda a explorar el universo
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Durante siglos, los científicos han aprendido que la luz existe en muchas formas que no podemos ver con nuestros ojos. Este rango de luz, llamado espectro electromagnético, incluye ondas de radio, rayos X y luz infrarroja. Cada tipo de luz muestra diferentes partes del universo.

**Qué pasó** – La astrofísica Emma Chapman explica cómo los telescopios modernos usan estas ondas invisibles para estudiar el espacio. Por ejemplo, el telescopio espacial James Webb observa la luz infrarroja para ver galaxias lejanas, mientras que el Square Kilometre Array, un enorme radiotelescopio en Australia, escuchará señales de las primeras estrellas y posibles señales de vida extraterrestre.

**Impacto** – Usar todo el espectro de luz permite a los científicos descubrir cosas invisibles para nuestros ojos, como plumas de agua en Europa, una luna de Júpiter, o galaxias que se formaron mucho más rápido de lo esperado. Esto ayuda a entender cómo comenzó el universo y dónde podría existir vida fuera de la Tierra.

**Próximo paso** – El Square Kilometre Array pronto comenzará a captar señales de radio más detalladas del espacio, permitiendo a los investigadores estudiar el universo temprano y buscar mensajes de otras civilizaciones. Mientras tanto, telescopios como James Webb seguirán revelando nuevos detalles sobre estrellas y planetas distantes.

**Resumen en una frase** – Al usar luz invisible más allá de lo que podemos ver, los científicos están descubriendo nuevas historias sobre el pasado del universo y la posibilidad de vida fuera de la Tierra.
articles.image_prompt: A warm, detailed painting of a vast cosmic landscape blending a spectrum of light waves—from deep radio waves depicted as gentle rippling waves stretching across a desert-like plain dotted with stylized radio telescope dishes, through vibrant bands of infrared and ultraviolet light arching overhead like colorful auroras, culminating in a radiant prism dispersing white starlight into a rainbow above a silhouetted, abstract representation of the universe’s first galaxies, all rendered in soft, natural earth tones and muted cosmic hues.

articles.title: Be a Clump Scout and Help Reveal Secrets of Stellar Nurseries
articles.summary: Help identify star-forming clumps in galaxy images, and help train machines to do the same.
articles.content:
In the mid-20th century, astronomers discovered strange "clumpy" galaxies filled with mysterious bright blobs – massive stellar nurseries where stars are born at an explosive rate. Curiously, these clumpy galaxies were much more common in the early universe than they are today. We still don’t know why they vanished.

The Euclid space telescope, an ESA (European Space Agency) mission with critical contributions from NASA, has begun to capture images of millions of galaxies. These images – far more than any team of professional scientists could ever catalog alone – include high-definition views of clumpy galaxies that promise to reveal structure within and among the clumps. Astronomers hope to use these images to obtain new information about which galaxies host clumps, where the clumps are, how and why they evolved, and more – but they need your help!

To tackle this mountain of data, scientists are creating a "digital assistant" in the form of machine learning, a kind of artificial intelligence. The machine algorithm has been partially trained with results from an earlier project called “Galaxy Zoo: Clump Scout.” Now, as a volunteer for the new Galaxy Zoo: Clump Scout II project, you’ll improve and train this tool further. You’ll examine images of galaxies that the machine has labelled with squares where it thinks it sees a real clump. The machine often gets confused by distant stars or camera glitches. So you’ll gently move those squares around, delete them, or add new ones, to help the algorithm learn.

As a part of Galaxy Zoo: Clump Scout II, you will help investigate how giant star-forming nurseries formed, solve the mystery of their disappearance over cosmic time, and reveal more about how star formation really works in galaxies. All you need is a laptop or smartphone. Click here to learn more!
articles.reasoning: The article describes a citizen science project that enables the public to contribute to meaningful astronomical research about star formation and galaxy evolution, leveraging AI to handle large datasets. This has broad scientific significance and public engagement, with clear context and impact on understanding the universe.
articles.personality_title: Join Clump Scout II to Help Study Star-Birth in Distant Galaxies
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – In space, some galaxies look 'clumpy' because they have bright, dense areas where many stars are born quickly. These clumpy galaxies were common long ago but are rare now. Scientists want to understand why.

**What happened** – The Euclid space telescope has started sending back millions of galaxy images, including many clumpy ones. Because there are too many images for scientists to study alone, they created a project called Galaxy Zoo: Clump Scout II. Volunteers can help by checking where a computer program thinks clumps are in the images and correcting it using a laptop or smartphone.

**Impact** – This project helps improve a smart computer program (artificial intelligence) that sorts galaxy images. By fixing the computer's guesses, volunteers help scientists learn how star nurseries form and why clumpy galaxies disappeared over time. It turns a huge amount of data into useful knowledge.

**What's next step** – As more people join and correct the computer’s work, the program will get better at finding star-forming clumps by itself. This will speed up discoveries about how galaxies change and how stars are made.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Anyone can help scientists study how stars are born in distant galaxies by improving an AI tool through the Galaxy Zoo: Clump Scout II project.
articles.personality_title_fr: Devenez Clump Scout II pour étudier la naissance des étoiles dans les galaxies lointaines
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Dans l’espace, certaines galaxies ont un aspect 'grumeleux' à cause de zones brillantes et denses où naissent rapidement beaucoup d’étoiles. Ces galaxies grumeleuses étaient fréquentes autrefois, mais sont rares aujourd’hui. Les scientifiques veulent comprendre pourquoi.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Le télescope spatial Euclid a commencé à envoyer des millions d’images de galaxies, y compris beaucoup de galaxies grumeleuses. Comme il y a trop d’images pour que les scientifiques les étudient seuls, ils ont créé un projet appelé Galaxy Zoo : Clump Scout II. Les volontaires peuvent aider en vérifiant où un programme informatique pense voir des grumeaux dans les images et en corrigeant ces emplacements avec un ordinateur ou un smartphone.

**Impact** – Ce projet aide à améliorer un programme informatique intelligent (intelligence artificielle) qui trie les images de galaxies. En corrigeant les erreurs de l’ordinateur, les volontaires aident les scientifiques à comprendre comment se forment les pépinières d’étoiles et pourquoi les galaxies grumeleuses ont disparu avec le temps. Cela transforme une énorme quantité de données en connaissances utiles.

**Prochaine étape** – Au fur et à mesure que plus de personnes participent et corrigent le travail de l’ordinateur, le programme deviendra meilleur pour détecter seul les grumeaux formant des étoiles. Cela accélérera les découvertes sur l’évolution des galaxies et la formation des étoiles.

**Une phrase clé** – Tout le monde peut aider les scientifiques à étudier la naissance des étoiles dans les galaxies lointaines en améliorant un outil d’intelligence artificielle grâce au projet Galaxy Zoo : Clump Scout II.
articles.personality_title_es: Únete a Clump Scout II para estudiar el nacimiento de estrellas en galaxias lejanas
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – En el espacio, algunas galaxias parecen 'grumosas' porque tienen áreas brillantes y densas donde nacen muchas estrellas rápidamente. Estas galaxias grumosas eran comunes hace mucho tiempo, pero ahora son raras. Los científicos quieren entender por qué.

**Qué pasó** – El telescopio espacial Euclid ha comenzado a enviar millones de imágenes de galaxias, incluyendo muchas grumosas. Como hay demasiadas imágenes para que los científicos las estudien solos, crearon un proyecto llamado Galaxy Zoo: Clump Scout II. Los voluntarios pueden ayudar revisando dónde un programa de computadora cree ver grumos en las imágenes y corrigiéndolos usando un portátil o teléfono.

**Impacto** – Este proyecto ayuda a mejorar un programa inteligente (inteligencia artificial) que organiza imágenes de galaxias. Corrigiendo los errores de la computadora, los voluntarios ayudan a los científicos a aprender cómo se forman los viveros de estrellas y por qué las galaxias grumosas desaparecieron con el tiempo. Convierte una gran cantidad de datos en conocimiento útil.

**Próximo paso** – A medida que más personas se unan y corrijan el trabajo de la computadora, el programa mejorará para encontrar grumos formadores de estrellas por sí solo. Esto acelerará los descubrimientos sobre cómo cambian las galaxias y cómo se forman las estrellas.

**Una frase clave** – Cualquier persona puede ayudar a los científicos a estudiar cómo nacen las estrellas en galaxias lejanas mejorando una herramienta de inteligencia artificial a través del proyecto Galaxy Zoo: Clump Scout II.
articles.image_prompt: A warm, detailed painting of a vast cosmic nursery filled with glowing, clustered star-forming blobs inside swirling, clumpy galaxies, overlaid with soft, translucent squares gently shifting and highlighting the bright stellar nurseries, symbolizing the collaborative effort of human and machine learning to uncover cosmic mysteries.
